elements.sent-2411-001: This paper establishes the first almost sure convergence rate and the first maximal concentration bound with exponential tails for general contractive stochastic approximation algorithms with Markovian noise.
elements.sent-2411-002: As a corollary, we also obtain convergence rates in $L^p$.
elements.sent-2411-003: Key to our successes is a novel discretization of the mean ODE of stochastic approximation algorithms using intervals with diminishing (instead of constant) length.
elements.sent-2411-004: As applications, we provide the first almost sure convergence rate for $Q$-learning with Markovian samples without count-based learning rates.
elements.sent-2411-005: We also provide the first concentration bound for off-policy temporal difference learning with Markovian samples.
elements.sent-2411-006: Stochastic approximation refers to a class of algorithms that update some weight vector iteratively and stochastically \citep{robbins1951stochastic,benveniste1990MP,kushner2003stochastic,borkar2009stochastic .}
elements.sent-2411-007: Notable stochastic approximation algorithms include stochastic gradient descent (SGD, \citet{kiefer1952Stochastic ), $Q$-learning \citep{watkins1989learning,watkins1992q , and temporal difference learning (TD, \citet{sutton1988learning ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Typically, a stochastic approximation algorithm adopts the following form \begin{equation}\label{eq sa update} \tag{SA} w_{t+1} = w_t + \alpha_t (H(w_t, Y_{t+1}) - w_t).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{eq sa update} \tag{SA} w_{t+1} = w_t + \alpha_t (H(w_t, Y_{t+1}) - w_t).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-009: Here $\qty{w_t \in \R^d}$ is the stochastic iterates, $\qty{\alpha_t}$ is a sequence of learning rates, $\qty{Y_t}$ is a sequence of random noise evolving in some space $\fY$, and $H: \R^d \times \fY \to \R^d$ is the function that generates the incremental update.
elements.sent-2411-010: The algorithm~\eqref{eq sa update can be viewed as a generalization of the running average.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Indeed, if $H(w, y)$ is independent of $w$, the learning rate $\alpha_t$ has the form of $\alpha_t = 1/(t+1)$, and $\qty{Y_t}$ are i.i.d., then $w_t$ can be regarded as the running average for computing $\E\qty[H(Y_t)]$.
elements.sent-2411-012: The law of large numbers (LLN) guarantees the almost sure convergence of a running average to the true expectation.

elements.sent-2411-082: In its simplest and most straightforward form, $Q$-learning performs the following updates to get the stochastic iterates $\qty{q_t}$ as \begin{equation}q_{t+1}(s, a) = \textstyle q_t(s, a) + \alpha_t (R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} q_t(S_{t+1}, a') - q_t(s, a)) \qq{if $(s, a) = (S_t, A_t)$} q_{t+1}(s, a) = q_t(s, a) \qq{otherwise.}\end{equation} This update rule can be written in a compact form as \begin{equation}\label{eq q learning} \tag{$Q$-learning} q_{t+1} = q_t + \alpha_t(H(q_t, (S_t, A_t, S_{t+1})) - q_t) \qty[H(q, (s_0, a_0, s_1))](s, a) \doteq \textstyle q(s, a) + \mathbbm{1}_{(s, a) = (s_0, a_0)}(r(s_0, a_0) + \gamma \max_{a_1} q(s_1, a_1) - q(s_0, a_0)),\end{equation} where $\mathbbm{1}$ is the indicator function.
